subject: Civics (Social and Political Life - II)
chapter: Growing Up as Boys and Girls

activities_projects.title: The Toy Box Categorization
activities_projects.objective: Identify gender stereotypes in childhood play
materials: Pictures of toys (cars, dolls, kitchen sets, cricket bats)
steps: Look at the list of toys and decide which ones are usually given to boys and which to girls
Paste the pictures on the respective charts
Discuss if a boy can play with a doll or a girl with a car
Write one sentence on why all toys should be for everyone
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes how society uses toys to tell children they have different futures
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to identify and question traditional toy categories (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Samoan Islands Case Study
activities_projects.objective: Learn about different ways of growing up
steps: Locate the Samoan Islands in the Pacific Ocean on the map
Read the account of children in the 1920s from the book
List two things Samoan children did (e.g., fishing, looking after siblings)
Identify at what age boys went on outdoor fishing trips
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that 'growing up' looks different in different cultures
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct facts about the Samoan childhood experience (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'Invisible Work' List
steps: Observe your mother or female family member for one morning
List every single task they do (e.g., making tea, cleaning, waking others up)
Check if these tasks are considered 'work' or just 'taken for granted'
Discuss if this work is physically demanding or easy
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Realizes that housework involves many tasks and is time-consuming
activities_projects.evaluation: Completeness of the task list and observation depth (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Madhya Pradesh School Comparison
activities_projects.objective: Understand gender differences in school design
steps: Read the 1960s story of schools in Madhya Pradesh from the book
Draw the girls' school with its central courtyard (seclusion)
Draw the boys' school with its open playground (public space)
Explain why the girls used the streets only as a means to get home quickly
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies how physical spaces are designed differently for boys and girls
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct depiction of the two school types and their purposes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Valuing the Housewife Roleplay
activities_projects.objective: Empathize with those who perform domestic labor
steps: Roleplay the scene where the family thinks the mother 'does not work'
Act out what happens when the mother goes on strike for a day
List the chaos that occurs when nobody is there to cook or clean
End the play with the family thanking the mother
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the concept of 'invisible labor' and its importance
activities_projects.evaluation: Participation in the roleplay and the message conveyed (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Domestic Workers: A Thank You Note
activities_projects.objective: Acknowledge the lives of domestic helpers
materials: Story of Melani from the book
steps: Read the story of Melani, a domestic worker in Delhi
Note down her daily routine (waking up at 5 AM, eating cold rotis)
Write a short thank you note to someone who helps clean your house or school
List one way we can show respect to domestic workers
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Sensitizes students to the hard work and low wages of domestic helpers
activities_projects.evaluation: Thoughtfulness of the note and understanding of Melani's story (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Care-giving Concept Map
activities_projects.objective: Define care-giving and its gender link
steps: Write 'Care-giving' in the center of the chart
Add branches like: Looking after the sick, Cooking, Teaching, Cleaning
Note that society often links these tasks primarily to women
Discuss why care-giving work is often valued less than office work
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains why domestic and care-giving work is 'devalued'
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the concept map and the explanation of devaluation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Anganwadi Visit / Research
activities_projects.objective: Learn about government steps for equality
steps: Find out what an 'Anganwadi' is from the textbook or teacher
Write down that the government sets these up in villages
Identify that the government passed laws for 'Crèches' in organizations with over 30 women
Explain how a crèche helps women go to work
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies government measures to support women working outside the home
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct understanding of Anganwadis and crèche laws (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Double Burden Illustration
activities_projects.objective: Visualize the workload of working women
steps: Draw a woman carrying a laptop/tools in one hand and a cooking pot in the other
Label this as the 'Double Burden'
Define 'Double Burden' in one simple sentence
Discuss if men in the family can help share this burden
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Grasps the concept that many women work both inside and outside the home
activities_projects.evaluation: Creative illustration and correct definition of the term (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Equality Slogan Writing
activities_projects.objective: Promote gender equality in the house
steps: Think of a slogan like 'Work has no Gender' or 'Help at Home, Share the Load'
Write it beautifully on the paper strip
Present it to the class and explain why it is important for brothers and sisters to help equally
Stick the slogans on the class bulletin board
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Encourages a positive attitude toward sharing domestic responsibilities
activities_projects.evaluation: Creativity and relevance of the slogan (10 marks)
